0: <title>Economic and environmental assessment of U.S. broiler production: opportunities to improve sustainability </title>
Colin M. Beal, David M. Robinson, * Jack Smith * Léda Gerber Van Doren, George T. Tabler, Samuel J. Rochell § Michael T. Kidd, Walter G. Bottje, and Xingen Lei

*B&D Engineering and Consulting LLC, Lander, WY 82520, USA; t University of Hawaii at Hilo, Pacific Aquaculture & Coastal Resources Center, College of Agriculture, Forestry, and Natural Resource Management, Hilo, HI 95720, USA; I University of Tennessee, Animal Science Department, Middle Tennessee AgResearch and Education Center, Spring Hill, TN 37174, USA; § Auburn University, Auburn, AL 36849, USA; Poultry Science Department, University of Arkansas, POSC O-114, Fayetteville, AR 72701, USA; and Animal Science Department, Cornell University, Ithaca, NY 14853, USA

ABSTRACT The United States is the largest broiler producer in the world, and Americans consume about 45 kg of chicken per capita per year, which generates sub- stantial economic and environmental footprints. We con- duct techno-economic analysis and life cycle assessment (TEA/LCA) to evaluate the sustainability performance of the U.S. broiler industry and quantify the cost, green- house gas (GHG) emissions, energy, water, land, fertil- izer, and respiratory impacts of 7 broiler production scenarios for a contract Grower, Integrator, and Com- bined control volume. The assessment is a farm-gate to farm-gate analysis that includes capital cost of chicken houses, labor, chicks brought into the farm, feeds, on-site fuels, and on-site emissions. We found that economics for the Integrator are profitable and dominated by the cost

of corn and soybean meal feeds, payments to the Grower, and revenue from live broilers. Additionally, we found that economics for the Grower generate modest return on investment (ROI) largely based on the cost of houses and labor when compared to contract revenue from the Integrator. Environmental impacts for GHG, energy, and respiratory effects are primarily associated with upstream feed production (roughly 65%-80% of total impacts) and on-site fuel consumption (~20%-35% of total impacts), while those for water, land, and eutrophication are almost entirely attributable to upstream feed production (litter spreading has a low economic allocation factor). Tradeoffs among sustainability metrics are further explored with a sensitivity analysis and by evaluating cost/environmental benefit scenarios.
Key words: life cycle assessment, techno-economic analysis, broiler production, scenario analysis, sustainability

Colin M. Beal, David M. Robinson, * Jack Smith * Léda Gerber Van Doren, George T. Tabler, Samuel J. Rochell § Michael T. Kidd, Walter G. Bottje, and Xingen Lei

3: Americans consume about 45 kg (100 lbs) of broiler meat (retail weight) per capita per year, which is roughly twice as much as either beef or pork (US Depart- ment of Agriculture, E. R. S., 2022). To meet this demand, along with exports, the U.S. broiler industry produced 27 billion kg (59 billion lbs) of broiler live weight in 2021 that was valued at over 31 billion dollars (US Department of Agriculture N. A. S. S., 2022). The broilers include a range of sizes with roughly 18% weigh- ing less than 4.25 lbs, 28% weighing between 4.25 and

6.25 lbs, 28% weighing 6.25 to 7.75 lbs, and 26% weigh- ing more than 7.75 lbs (US Department of Agriculture, A. M. S., 2023). Broiler meat serves as an important source of lean protein and nutrients, and the industry has been estimated to provide over one million jobs (John Dunham & Associates, 2022). However, there are concerns about the sustainability of the commercial broiler industry (and of the corresponding feed industry) with respect to resource impacts on land, water, energy, and fertilizer, as well as environmental impacts for greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, eutrophication, and human health. To evaluate these sustainability trade- offs, this study presents a techno-economic analysis and life cycle assessment (TEA/LCA) of 7 commercially relevant broiler production systems.

© 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc. on behalf of Poultry Science Association Inc. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/ 4.0/). Received March 2, 2023. Accepted June 16, 2023. 1 Corresponding author: info@tea-lca.com

While there have been a large number of life cycle assessments published regarding poultry production (de Vries and de Boer, 2010; Lesschen et al., 2011; MEDA et al., 2011; FAO, 2013; Prudêncio da Silva et al., 2014;

Benavides et al., 2020; Costantini et al., 2021; Bos et al., 2022; Kiss et al., 2022a,b; Mostert et al., 2022; Yayli and Kilic, 2023; Zisis et al., 2023), only a few of them analyze the U.S. commercial industry (Pelletier, 2008; Putman et al., 2017; Thoma, 2021) and those that include corre- sponding economic impacts (i.e., TEA) are for other coun- tries (Heidari et al., 2011; Castellini et al., 2012). Similarly, while there have been economic assessments presented for broiler production (Heien, 1976; Sheppard, 2004; Firdaus and Komalasari, 2010; AL-Sharafat and Al-fawwaz, 2013; Udo et al., 2017; van Horne, 2020), few are applicable to the U.S. commercial industry. One challenge with analyzing the U.S. broiler industry, which is addressed in this study, is the unique arrangement between the ~20 large poultry companies that account for >95% of broiler production (MacDonald, 2014) (Integrators) and the small producers who are contracted to raise the birds (Growers). This arrangement adds complexity to defining the control volume and attributing economic and environmental burdens for a TEA/LCA. However, because the U.S. broiler industry is the largest in the world (producing roughly 20% of the world's chicken (FAO, 2021)), it is important to accurately quantify the sector's sustainability performance. Further- more, because broiler production methods and government policies are driven by economic and environmental consider- ations, it is critical to evaluate these impacts simultaneously and consistently. To meet these needs, the TEA/LCA in this study is designed to characterize the commercial U.S. broiler industry and evaluate how different production sys tems and management practices (scenarios) can influence overall sustainability tradeoffs. In addition to a Baseline Case scenario, which reflects industry-average performance, we include alternative pro- duction scenarios to evaluate the impact of low-impact corn (that we call "sustainable corn"), renewable energy (RWN EN), larger farms, longer growing periods, and an alterna- tive cooling method. A sensitivity analysis is included to demonstrate the potential range of results depending on system performance, broiler house lifespan, economic condi- tions, and life cycle impacts. The results are presented in the main body of the paper for a Combined control volume that includes Growers and Integrators to evaluate the overall production system. The supplemental information (SI) includes results that are specific to the Grower's control vol- ume and the Integrator's control volume.

7: The primary objective of this study is to compare the sustainability performance of different broiler produc- tion systems using the same model. Effort was taken to ensure that the Baseline Case (BC) model accurately reflects the commercial broiler industry in the United States (Hoppe, 2014; MacDonald, 2014; Putman et al., 2017) The BC (described in detail below) models a farm with 4 houses that produces 2.5 kg (5.5 lb) chickens using conventional sources of electricity, propane, and diesel, as well as conventional feed ingredients and evap- orative cool-cell cooling. Results are presented for the

net present value (NPV) of each production system after 30 years of operation ($), operating cost of produc- tion ($/kg live weight), GHG emissions (kg COe/kg live weight), cumulative (fossil) energy demand (CED, MJ/kg live weight), water depletion potential (WDP, m³ /kg live weight), agricultural land use (ALOP, kg live weight), eutrophication (EUT, kg N-eq/kg live weight), and respiratory effects (RE, kg PM2.5-eq/kg live weight), as defined below. The functional unit for this study is 1 kg of live broiler body weight (BW). Alternate cases include:

<list>- Large farm (LF)-models a poultry farm with 10 houses 
- Large bird (LB)-models the production of 3.8 kg (8.4 lbs) broilers 
- Renewable energy (RWN I)-models electricity and heat provided by solar power and on-site diesel fuel being replaced with biodiesel 
- Sustainable corn (SC)-The BC uses a standard cost of corn ($5.40/bu or $0.21/kg) and life cycle inven- tory impacts for corn that are obtained from ecoin- vent (Wernet et al., 2016), while the SC case uses costs and inventory impacts that are determined from a stand-alone corn production model (Beal et al., 2021) that implements more sustainable practi- ces. The SC case is intended to evaluate how cost and environmental impacts of upstream corn production impact those for broiler production, and how these impacts change with improved upstream cultivation practices. Specifically, the scenario models the use of corn that is produced without irrigation, and with 50% lower synthetic fertilizer, lime, herbicide, and insecticide rates than conventional corn. It also repla- ces conventional diesel fuel used on the corn farm with biodiesel. The yield of sustainable corn (9.3 ha) is assumed to be 75% of the conventional corn yield (11.4 t/ha). The characteristics of both corn types are listed in Table 1. 
- High Temperature (HT) sprinkler cooling-models a scenario with higher-temperature houses (31°C or 88° F as compared to 29°C or 85°F in BC) that use large- droplet sprinklers to cool broilers (Liang et al., 2014, 2020), resulting in a lower input of water (64% lower</list>
Table 1. Cost and environmental impacts for conventional and sustainable corn.

|Corn|Sustainable corn Cost ($/kg)|$0.21|$0.29 IPCC 2013 100a GHG1 CO2e/kg)|427|181 CED² Fossil (MJ/kg)|3.64|2.27 WDP3 (m³ /kg)|0.245|0.002 ALOP4 (m ²/kg)|0.650|0.867 EUT5 (kg N-eq/kg)|0.00096|0.00048 RE6 (kg PM2.5-eq/kg)|0.00050|0.00025

Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change 100-year Greenhouse Gas Impact. Cumulative Energy Demand. Superscript(3)Water Depletion Potential. Agricultural Land Occupation. Eutrophication. 6Respiratory Effects.
than BC) and lower feed conversion ratio (1.80 kg feed/kg gain, which is 3.2% lower than BC).

Low-GHG (LG) models several adjustments to reduce life cycle GHG emissions, including renewable energy as in RE, large droplet sprinklers and improved feed efficiency as in HT, and sustainable corn as in SC.

Effort was taken to ensure that the Baseline Case (BC) model accurately reflects the commercial broiler industry in the United States (Hoppe, 2014; MacDonald, 2014; Putman et al., 2017) The BC (described in detail below) models a farm with 4 houses that produces 2.5 kg (5.5 lb) chickens using conventional sources of electricity, propane, and diesel, as well as conventional feed ingredients and evap- orative cool-cell cooling. Results are presented for the

net present value (NPV) of each production system after 30 years of operation ($), operating cost of produc- tion ($/kg live weight), GHG emissions (kg COe/kg live weight), cumulative (fossil) energy demand (CED, MJ/kg live weight), water depletion potential (WDP, m³ /kg live weight), agricultural land use (ALOP, kg live weight), eutrophication (EUT, kg N-eq/kg live weight), and respiratory effects (RE, kg PM2.5-eq/kg live weight), as defined below. The functional unit for this study is 1 kg of live broiler body weight (BW). Alternate cases include:

<list>- Large farm (LF)-models a poultry farm with 10 houses 
- Large bird (LB)-models the production of 3.8 kg (8.4 lbs) broilers 
- Renewable energy (RWN I)-models electricity and heat provided by solar power and on-site diesel fuel being replaced with biodiesel 
- Sustainable corn (SC)-The BC uses a standard cost of corn ($5.40/bu or $0.21/kg) and life cycle inven- tory impacts for corn that are obtained from ecoin- vent (Wernet et al., 2016), while the SC case uses costs and inventory impacts that are determined from a stand-alone corn production model (Beal et al., 2021) that implements more sustainable practi- ces. The SC case is intended to evaluate how cost and environmental impacts of upstream corn production impact those for broiler production, and how these impacts change with improved upstream cultivation practices. Specifically, the scenario models the use of corn that is produced without irrigation, and with 50% lower synthetic fertilizer, lime, herbicide, and insecticide rates than conventional corn. It also repla- ces conventional diesel fuel used on the corn farm with biodiesel. The yield of sustainable corn (9.3 ha) is assumed to be 75% of the conventional corn yield (11.4 t/ha). The characteristics of both corn types are listed in Table 1. 
- High Temperature (HT) sprinkler cooling-models a scenario with higher-temperature houses (31°C or 88° F as compared to 29°C or 85°F in BC) that use large- droplet sprinklers to cool broilers (Liang et al., 2014, 2020), resulting in a lower input of water (64% lower</list>
Table 1. Cost and environmental impacts for conventional and sustainable corn.

|Corn|Sustainable corn Cost ($/kg)|$0.21|$0.29 IPCC 2013 100a GHG1 CO2e/kg)|427|181 CED² Fossil (MJ/kg)|3.64|2.27 WDP3 (m³ /kg)|0.245|0.002 ALOP4 (m ²/kg)|0.650|0.867 EUT5 (kg N-eq/kg)|0.00096|0.00048 RE6 (kg PM2.5-eq/kg)|0.00050|0.00025

than BC) and lower feed conversion ratio (1.80 kg feed/kg gain, which is 3.2% lower than BC).

Low-GHG (LG) models several adjustments to reduce life cycle GHG emissions, including renewable energy as in RE, large droplet sprinklers and improved feed efficiency as in HT, and sustainable corn as in SC.

11: Figure 1 illustrates the system boundaries for the model, which are intended to represent the organization of the broiler industry in which ~97% of chickens are produced in "contract grower operations" (MacDonald, 2014). The system boundaries include distinct control volumes for the Grower (who owns and operates the houses) and the Integrator (who owns the birds and feed), and an aggregate Combined control volume that encompasses the overall costs and life cycle impacts for the entire broiler production system. The Combined control volume is a cradle to farm-exit-gate analysis, where chicks enter the Combined control volume as inputs and exit the Combine control volume as live broilers.

Chick production and feed production occur upstream from the Combined control volume. The (sub-) control volumes are assigned based on ownership and financial payments. The Grower owns and operates the broiler houses and is therefore assigned the costs and environ- mental impacts for items that they purchase to operate the farm, as well as the outputs within their control

(litter and emissions). The Integrator owns both the birds and the feed, and is therefore assigned the cost and environmental impacts associated with these inputs, as well as the outputs within their control (live broilers). This division is made to accurately attribute costs, reve- nues, and environmental impacts between the Grower and Integrator such that alternative scenarios can be evaluated with practical consideration of the commercial industry and assign responsibility for which participant can make potential improvements. The Combined con- trol volume includes the Grower and Integrator control volumes and the dashed lines indicate physical flows between the Integrator and Grower that do not cross the TEA/LCA control volumes (e.g., the feed never enters the Grower's TEA/LCA control volume because the grower has no ownership or control of this product).

13: Broiler Production Model The BC uses parameters that represent typical broiler farm operations to serve as a business-as-usual scenario for comparison with alter- native production scenarios. The TEA/LCA model is based on a simulated farm of four, 2,325 m² 2 (25,000 ft2 broiler houses in Arkansas and is representative of the commercial U.S. broiler industry (MacDonald, 2014). The BC model is based on a 65-d production cycle (47 d of growth with 18 d between flocks), yielding 5.69 cycles per year, with identical operations year-over-year. The number of broilers produced (Bp) is based on the number

of chicks received (Br) by the Grower (13.56 birds/m², 1.26 birds/ft2, or 31,500 birds/house in BC), the mortal- ity rate (MR, 3.63% in BC), and the number of cycles per year (C, 5.69 in BC) as (1)

In the BC, there are 690,351 broilers produced per year, yielding 1,725,879 kg of live weight per year (2.5 kg live weight/bird or 5.5 lbs live weight/bird). Feed Model Broilers in the baseline scenario receive a feed ration that consists of corn (55%), soybean meal (38%), corn oil (3%), dicalcium phosphate (2%), limestone (<1%), and other minor ingredients (Rochell, 2020). The amount of feed consumed is calculated based on the feed conversion rate (FCR) for each scenario, and the BC model assumes the national average across all birds (1.86 kg feed/kg gain (MacDonald, 2014)). The total amount of feed required (F) is calculated as

where WBP is the average final BW of a produced broiler (2.5 kg in the BC) and WBR is the average starting weight of a received chick (0.04 kg in the BC). Operations Model The broiler houses consume elec- tricity (0.78 MJ/kg BW) and propane (2.57 MJ/kg BW). Diesel fuel is estimated for transporting materials, operating a backup generator, and operating equipment (0.04 MJ/kg BW). Bedding is applied to the broiler house floor (0.04 kg/kg BW). Subsurface water for broilers to drink is consumed at a rate of 3.81 L/kg BW. Subsurface water is also consumed for cooling the broiler houses based on a cool-cell system (1.43 L/kg BW). Labor costs are not included in this analysis, as it is assumed that the Grower does not hire out day-to-day work. In the BC, 0.20 kg of litter is produced per kg of feed and it is assumed that all litter is sold for $42/t (MacDonald, 2014; Fairchild and Ritz, 2015; LPELC, 2019; Tabler, 2020).

Capital Costs The initial capital investment costs for the Grower include land (3.72 ha for $7,114/ha in Arkansas (US Department of Agriculture N. A. S. S., 2020)), broiler houses ($134.41/m2, or 12.50/sq-ft (MacDonald, 2014), for 2,325 m² houses), and an esti- mated equipment cost ($17,750/house for machinery, tools, etc.). Therefore, the total initial investment for 4 houses in the BC case is $1,347,463.

1: GHG accounting is based on guidelines from the IPCC (Gavrilova et al., 2019) and includes the following sour- ces of emissions: manure CH4, manure N2O, upstream emissions, and on-site diesel and propane combustion, as described below. For manure CH4 and manure N2O, the GHG accounting follows the Tier 2 methodology of the IPCC guidelines, although most emissions factors for these categories are not country-specific and there is overlap between the Tier 1 and 2 equations. While the sensitivity analysis (described below) does not specifi- cally address the potential range of emissions factors for different countries or different states within the United States, these impacts are captured in the sensitivity anal- ysis for FCR, which influences the amount of CH4 and N2O that are generated from manure. Biogenic carbon contained in feeds (originating from atmospheric CO2) is assumed to eventually be released back to the atmo- sphere as CO2 during respiration (broilers eating feed or humans eating chicken), manure degradation, or other pathways, and is therefore considered a net-zero impact. Emissions from litter spreading on soils are not included in the control volume as litter is considered a co-product.
Manure CH4 emissions (GHGmch4) are calculated according to IPCC Eq. 10.23 as MCF[-] (10)

where Bo is the maximum CH4 capacity (0.36), MCF is the methane conversion factor (0.015 for poultry), and G WP CH4 is the global warming potential for methane (27). Manure volatile solids (VS) are determined according to (11)
where VSF kg DM is the volatile solids conversion factor (0.13) according to IPCC Eq. 10.24.

Manure N2O emissions (GHGmn20) are calculated as the sum of direct (GHGmn20D), indirect volatile (GHGmn2ov), and indirect leaching (GHGmn20L) sources according to IPCC Eqs. 10.25 to 10.29 for emissions from the broiler houses. = GHGmn2oV + (12)

where NFD (equivalent to EF3) is the direct N2O emis- sions factor (0.001 for poultry houses), GWPN20 is the N2O global warming potential (273), and N is the total nitrogen excretion, which is calculated as

where PC is the protein content of feed, NE is the ratio of nitrogen excretion to nitrogen intake after accounting for nitrogen retained in the chickens (0.7), and NC is the nitrogen conversion factor for protein (6.25, correspond- ing to 16% of protein being nitrogen).
where FRAC gasms is 0.4 and NFv (equivalent to EF4) is the indirect volatile N2O emissions factor (0.005).
GHGmn20L are calculated as NFL 44 (16) kgN20-N
where FRACleachms is 0 for the poultry houses and NFL (equivalent to EF5) is the indirect leaching N2O emis- sions factor (0.011).

Upstream embedded GHG emissions (GHGu) include the emissions generated "upstream" from the control vol- ume for products imported to the system, such as pur- chased feeds, electricity, etc. These emissions are calculated as

where X is the amount of an imported product (e.g., kg corn/yr, MJ electricity/yr, etc.) and GHGx is the emis- sions impact of each product (e.g., kg CO2e/kg corn, kg CO2e/MJ electricity, etc.) reported in the ecoinvent life cycle assessment database (Wernet et al., 2016).
On-site emissions (GHG0) are generated from the combustion of diesel fuel and propane within the control volume and calculated as

where Y is the amount of fuel (MJ diesel/yr, MJ pro- pane/yr, etc.) and GHGy is the emissions impact of each fuel (e.g., kg CO2e/MJ diesel, kg CO2e/MJ pro- pane, etc.) reported in the ecoinvent life-cycle assess- ment database (Wernet et al., 2016). For the fuels, the overall GHG impact includes upstream emissions for producing the fuel combined with the on-site emissions from combustion (e.g., the GHG impact for on-farm die- sel combustion is 127 g CO2e/MJ).

The GHG emissions are allocated between the broiler live weight and litter based on economic allocation, assuming an average value of $0.97/kg BW ($0.44/lb BW), which yields relative allocation of 98% and 2%, respectively. Thus, the GHG emissions per kg of BW (GHG') are GHG (20)
where AFBW is the live body weight allocation factor (0.98) and Xcw is the amount of BW generated each year.

6: GHG accounting is based on guidelines from the IPCC (Gavrilova et al., 2019) and includes the following sour- ces of emissions: manure CH4, manure N2O, upstream emissions, and on-site diesel and propane combustion, as described below. For manure CH4 and manure N2O, the GHG accounting follows the Tier 2 methodology of the IPCC guidelines, although most emissions factors for these categories are not country-specific and there is overlap between the Tier 1 and 2 equations. While the sensitivity analysis (described below) does not specifi- cally address the potential range of emissions factors for different countries or different states within the United States, these impacts are captured in the sensitivity anal- ysis for FCR, which influences the amount of CH4 and N2O that are generated from manure. Biogenic carbon contained in feeds (originating from atmospheric CO2) is assumed to eventually be released back to the atmo- sphere as CO2 during respiration (broilers eating feed or humans eating chicken), manure degradation, or other pathways, and is therefore considered a net-zero impact. Emissions from litter spreading on soils are not included in the control volume as litter is considered a co-product.

8: A sensitivity analysis was conducted to understand the individual effects of the main technical parameters on the economic and environmental performances of the facility for the BC Combined control volume. The parameters evaluated in the sensitivity analysis are listed in Table 3.
9: Detailed results for the Combined control volume (Figure 1) are presented in this section, while corre- sponding results for the Integrator and Grower control volumes are included in the Supplemental Information. Section 3.3 provides an overview comparison of the TEA/LCA results for all 3 control volumes. In general, we find that the primary sources of cost and environ- mental impacts in broiler production are associated with feed (mainly corn and soybean meal), energy inputs, labor, and manure management.

1: <header>Techno-Economic Analysis Results for the Combined Control Volume </header>
Table 4 lists the TEA results and Figure 3 presents a contribution breakdown for operational expenses in all 7 cases relating to the Combined control volume.

Baseline Case (BC). The NPV of the system after 30 years of operation was determined to be $3.06M. This profit represents a 657% ROI (on the down payment, Ceq). During the first 10 years of operation, when loan payments and depreciation are applied, the cumulative discounted cash flow increases from -$404,200 (equity share of capital cost in year 0) to $1,005,100 (year 10). From year 11 to 30, annual gross revenue ($1.70M) exceeds annual operating costs ($1.33M) by $366,700,

resulting in $71,500 of annual tax payments and a steady annual cash flow of $268,800. The largest contrib- utors to the capital costs ($1,347,000) include broiler houses ($1,250,000), land ($26,000), and equipment ($60,000). When summing other costs over the entire 30-year facility lifetime, the largest operating costs include: soybean meal ($18.2M), corn ($11.4M), other feed ingredients ($5.5M), chicks ($0.8M), taxes ($1.8M), heat ($3.2M), electricity ($619,000), loan interest ($278,000), insurance ($396,000), and maintenance ($396,000), bedding ($251,000), and diesel ($45,000) for a total of $42.9M (Figure 2). The annual operating cost ($1.36M) corresponds to $0.77/kg ($0.35/lb) live weight. Revenues from broilers produced and litter sold over the 30-year facility life are $50.2M ($0.97/kg or

$0.45/lb live weight) and $826,000, respectively. The NPV could be increased by obtaining low-cost feed, reducing taxes and labor costs, reducing capital costs for constructing the houses, and/or implementing more effi- cient heating and cooling systems.

Large Farm Case (LF). Compared to the BC, the LF operation is 2 and a half times larger and intended to represent a larger contract operation that can generate better financial returns by enabling economies of scale (MacDonald, 2014). However, all model equations scale linearly with the number of broiler houses as compared to BC (2.5:1). The additional revenue obtained through

the production of more broilers provides a greater NPV ($7.65M) over 30 years. However, because capital invest- ment also increases by a factor of 2.5, the scenario attains the same ROI (657%) over the facility lifespan as the BC.

Large Bird Case (LB). Compared to the BC, the LB operation produces broilers that are approximately one and a half times larger. The initial investment remains the same for LB, but several input factors vary in order to produce larger birds. The LB case has fewer cycles per year (4.7), fewer birds per house (17,800), a higher mortality rate (3.8%), and a higher FCR (2.1 kg

Figure 3. Annual operating expenses and contribution breakdown for each case. Abbreviations: BC, Baseline Case; HT, High Temperature case; LB, Large Bird scenario; LF, Large Farm scenario; LG, Low GHG case; RWN EN, Renewable Energy case; SC, Sustainable Corn scenario.

feed/kg gain). These input factors increase the operating costs to $0.86/kg live weight and reduce the annual yield of broiler live weight by 5.5% (1,626,000 kg/yr). How- ever, the large birds receive a price that is 9% higher than in the BC ($1.06/kg or $0.48/lb), which nearly off- sets the increased production cost. The LB case yields a 30-year NPV of $2,940,000, which is 4% less than the BC and yields a 627% return on investment.

Renewable Energy Case (RWN EN). The RWN EN operation models all on-farm energy inputs from renewable sources, whereby solar power is used for elec- trical and heat requirements and biodiesel replaces con- ventional diesel. The cost of solar power ($0.021/MJ) is based on community scale PV power (Lazard, 2021) and it is assumed that the same amount of heat is required in RWN EN as BC (4,400 GJ/yr). The initial investment is assumed to be the same for the RWN EN case as compared to the BC because the renewable energy is purchased from off-site. Grower operational costs (which include Electricity, Heat, Diesel, and Bedding) total $136,000/yr for BC and $132,000/yr for RWN EN. Though small, this difference in annual energy cost requirements reduces the operating cost for the RWN EN scenario to $0.771/kg live weight (rather than $0.773/kg live weight, as in BC). Because both BC and RWN EN produce the same amount (1,726,000 kg) of live weight per year, RWN EN has a favorable economic outcome when compared to BC. The NPV after 30 years to $3,114,000, or a 670% ROI. The seemingly modest decrease in production cost ($0.002/kg live weight) creates a more profitable scenario over time and reduces environmental impacts (discussed below). This scenario has high sensitivity to the assumed cost of conventional pro- pane and solar-based heat: a 10% change in the cost of conventional propane or solar energy can reverse the economic comparison between the 2 scenarios.

Sustainable Corn Case (SC). The initial invest- ment, broiler performance, and revenue remain the same for SC as for BC, but the increased operating cost for purchasing sustainable corn ($0.29/kg VS. $0.21/kg) reduces the 30-year NPV to $1,356,000, or only a 236% ROI. Due to the increased cost of corn, the cost of pro- duction is increased to $0.85/kg live weight.

High Temperature Sprinkler Cooling Case (HT). Compared to the BC, the HT operation models broiler houses with large-drop sprinkler cooling systems to regulate bird temperature. All of the model variables are the same as BC except for cooling water consump- tion which is reduced by 65% as compared to the cool- cell (0.51 kg H2O/kg live weight), and feed conversion ratio, which is improved by 3.2% (1.80 kg feed/kg live weight). The difference in capital cost for the sprinklers as compared to the cool-cell system is neglected for this study. The improved feed efficiency has the largest impact on the economics of HT, yielding a NPV after 30 years of $3,511,000 or a 769% ROI. The operating cost of HT is reduced to $0.75/kg live weight due to reduced feed costs (cooling water is assumed to be free and obtained from a subsurface well on-site).

Low-GHG Case (LG). Compared to the BC, the LG operation models several adjustments intended to reduce life cycle GHG emissions of broiler production. LG combines the adjustments from the RE, SC, and HT cases to minimize operational GHG emissions, reduce upstream environmental impacts from corn, and improve feed efficiency. By combining all of these practi- ces, the operating cost of LG is $0.83/kg live weight, which yields a 30-year NPV of $1,927,000, or a 377% ROI. Similar to RE and SC, there are environmental benefits (discussed below). Though the LG scenario does not net the same profit as the BC scenario, increased sales price could be achieved by marketing "sustainable chicken" with reduced GHG emissions and selling the product at a premium sales price, similar to other certi- fied food products (Where Food Comes From Inc, 2021). Alternatively, revenue from GHG reduction credits could incentivize producers to implement the low-GHG practices.
2: <header>Techno-Economic Analysis Results for the Combined Control Volume </header>
Table 4 lists the TEA results and Figure 3 presents a contribution breakdown for operational expenses in all 7 cases relating to the Combined control volume.

Baseline Case (BC). The NPV of the system after 30 years of operation was determined to be $3.06M. This profit represents a 657% ROI (on the down payment, Ceq). During the first 10 years of operation, when loan payments and depreciation are applied, the cumulative discounted cash flow increases from -$404,200 (equity share of capital cost in year 0) to $1,005,100 (year 10). resulting in $71,500 of annual tax payments and a steady annual cash flow of $268,800. $0.45/lb live weight) and $826,000, respectively. The NPV could be increased by obtaining low-cost feed, reducing taxes and labor costs, reducing capital costs for constructing the houses, and/or implementing more effi- cient heating and cooling systems.

Large Farm Case (LF). Compared to the BC, the LF operation is 2 and a half times larger and intended to represent a larger contract operation that can generate better financial returns by enabling economies of scale (MacDonald, 2014). However, all model equations scale linearly with the number of broiler houses as compared to BC (2.5:1). The additional revenue obtained through

the production of more broilers provides a greater NPV ($7.65M) over 30 years. However, because capital invest- ment also increases by a factor of 2.5, the scenario attains the same ROI (657%) over the facility lifespan as the BC.

Large Bird Case (LB). Compared to the BC, the LB operation produces broilers that are approximately one and a half times larger. The initial investment remains the same for LB, but several input factors vary in order to produce larger birds. The LB case has fewer cycles per year (4.7), fewer birds per house (17,800), a higher mortality rate (3.8%), and a higher FCR (2.1 kg

Figure 3. Annual operating expenses and contribution breakdown for each case. Abbreviations: BC, Baseline Case; HT, High Temperature case; LB, Large Bird scenario; LF, Large Farm scenario; LG, Low GHG case; RWN EN, Renewable Energy case; SC, Sustainable Corn scenario.

feed/kg gain). These input factors increase the operating costs to $0.86/kg live weight and reduce the annual yield of broiler live weight by 5.5% (1,626,000 kg/yr). How- ever, the large birds receive a price that is 9% higher than in the BC ($1.06/kg or $0.48/lb), which nearly off- sets the increased production cost. The LB case yields a 30-year NPV of $2,940,000, which is 4% less than the BC and yields a 627% return on investment.

Renewable Energy Case (RWN EN). The RWN EN operation models all on-farm energy inputs from renewable sources, whereby solar power is used for elec- trical and heat requirements and biodiesel replaces con- ventional diesel. The cost of solar power ($0.021/MJ) is based on community scale PV power (Lazard, 2021) and it is assumed that the same amount of heat is required in RWN EN as BC (4,400 GJ/yr). The initial investment is assumed to be the same for the RWN EN case as compared to the BC because the renewable energy is purchased from off-site. Grower operational costs (which include Electricity, Heat, Diesel, and Bedding) total $136,000/yr for BC and $132,000/yr for RWN EN. Though small, this difference in annual energy cost requirements reduces the operating cost for the RWN EN scenario to $0.771/kg live weight (rather than $0.773/kg live weight, as in BC). Because both BC and RWN EN produce the same amount (1,726,000 kg) of live weight per year, RWN EN has a favorable economic outcome when compared to BC. The NPV after 30 years to $3,114,000, or a 670% ROI. The seemingly modest decrease in production cost ($0.002/kg live weight) creates a more profitable scenario over time and reduces environmental impacts (discussed below). This scenario has high sensitivity to the assumed cost of conventional pro- pane and solar-based heat: a 10% change in the cost of conventional propane or solar energy can reverse the economic comparison between the 2 scenarios.

Sustainable Corn Case (SC). The initial invest- ment, broiler performance, and revenue remain the same for SC as for BC, but the increased operating cost for purchasing sustainable corn ($0.29/kg VS. $0.21/kg) reduces the 30-year NPV to $1,356,000, or only a 236% ROI. Due to the increased cost of corn, the cost of pro- duction is increased to $0.85/kg live weight.

High Temperature Sprinkler Cooling Case (HT). Compared to the BC, the HT operation models broiler houses with large-drop sprinkler cooling systems to regulate bird temperature. All of the model variables are the same as BC except for cooling water consump- tion which is reduced by 65% as compared to the cool- cell (0.51 kg H2O/kg live weight), and feed conversion ratio, which is improved by 3.2% (1.80 kg feed/kg live weight). The difference in capital cost for the sprinklers as compared to the cool-cell system is neglected for this study. The improved feed efficiency has the largest impact on the economics of HT, yielding a NPV after 30 years of $3,511,000 or a 769% ROI. The operating cost of HT is reduced to $0.75/kg live weight due to reduced feed costs (cooling water is assumed to be free and obtained from a subsurface well on-site).

Low-GHG Case (LG). Compared to the BC, the LG operation models several adjustments intended to reduce life cycle GHG emissions of broiler production. LG combines the adjustments from the RE, SC, and HT cases to minimize operational GHG emissions, reduce upstream environmental impacts from corn, and improve feed efficiency. By combining all of these practi- ces, the operating cost of LG is $0.83/kg live weight, which yields a 30-year NPV of $1,927,000, or a 377% ROI. Similar to RE and SC, there are environmental benefits (discussed below). Though the LG scenario does not net the same profit as the BC scenario, increased sales price could be achieved by marketing "sustainable chicken" with reduced GHG emissions and selling the product at a premium sales price, similar to other certi- fied food products (Where Food Comes From Inc, 2021). Alternatively, revenue from GHG reduction credits could incentivize producers to implement the low-GHG practices.

$0.45/lb live weight) and $826,000, respectively. The NPV could be increased by obtaining low-cost feed, reducing taxes and labor costs, reducing capital costs for constructing the houses, and/or implementing more effi- cient heating and cooling systems.

Large Farm Case (LF). Compared to the BC, the LF operation is 2 and a half times larger and intended to represent a larger contract operation that can generate better financial returns by enabling economies of scale (MacDonald, 2014). However, all model equations scale linearly with the number of broiler houses as compared to BC (2.5:1). The additional revenue obtained through

the production of more broilers provides a greater NPV ($7.65M) over 30 years. However, because capital invest- ment also increases by a factor of 2.5, the scenario attains the same ROI (657%) over the facility lifespan as the BC.

Large Bird Case (LB). Compared to the BC, the LB operation produces broilers that are approximately one and a half times larger. The initial investment remains the same for LB, but several input factors vary in order to produce larger birds. The LB case has fewer cycles per year (4.7), fewer birds per house (17,800), a higher mortality rate (3.8%), and a higher FCR (2.1 kg

Figure 3. Annual operating expenses and contribution breakdown for each case. Abbreviations: BC, Baseline Case; HT, High Temperature case; LB, Large Bird scenario; LF, Large Farm scenario; LG, Low GHG case; RWN EN, Renewable Energy case; SC, Sustainable Corn scenario.

feed/kg gain). These input factors increase the operating costs to $0.86/kg live weight and reduce the annual yield of broiler live weight by 5.5% (1,626,000 kg/yr). How- ever, the large birds receive a price that is 9% higher than in the BC ($1.06/kg or $0.48/lb), which nearly off- sets the increased production cost. The LB case yields a 30-year NPV of $2,940,000, which is 4% less than the BC and yields a 627% return on investment.

Renewable Energy Case (RWN EN). The RWN EN operation models all on-farm energy inputs from renewable sources, whereby solar power is used for elec- trical and heat requirements and biodiesel replaces con- ventional diesel. The cost of solar power ($0.021/MJ) is based on community scale PV power (Lazard, 2021) and it is assumed that the same amount of heat is required in RWN EN as BC (4,400 GJ/yr). The initial investment is assumed to be the same for the RWN EN case as compared to the BC because the renewable energy is purchased from off-site. Grower operational costs (which include Electricity, Heat, Diesel, and Bedding) total $136,000/yr for BC and $132,000/yr for RWN EN. Though small, this difference in annual energy cost requirements reduces the operating cost for the RWN EN scenario to $0.771/kg live weight (rather than $0.773/kg live weight, as in BC). Because both BC and RWN EN produce the same amount (1,726,000 kg) of live weight per year, RWN EN has a favorable economic outcome when compared to BC. The NPV after 30 years to $3,114,000, or a 670% ROI. The seemingly modest decrease in production cost ($0.002/kg live weight) creates a more profitable scenario over time and reduces environmental impacts (discussed below). This scenario has high sensitivity to the assumed cost of conventional pro- pane and solar-based heat: a 10% change in the cost of conventional propane or solar energy can reverse the economic comparison between the 2 scenarios.

Sustainable Corn Case (SC). The initial invest- ment, broiler performance, and revenue remain the same for SC as for BC, but the increased operating cost for purchasing sustainable corn ($0.29/kg VS. $0.21/kg) reduces the 30-year NPV to $1,356,000, or only a 236% ROI. Due to the increased cost of corn, the cost of pro- duction is increased to $0.85/kg live weight.

High Temperature Sprinkler Cooling Case (HT). Compared to the BC, the HT operation models broiler houses with large-drop sprinkler cooling systems to regulate bird temperature. All of the model variables are the same as BC except for cooling water consump- tion which is reduced by 65% as compared to the cool- cell (0.51 kg H2O/kg live weight), and feed conversion ratio, which is improved by 3.2% (1.80 kg feed/kg live weight). The difference in capital cost for the sprinklers as compared to the cool-cell system is neglected for this study. The improved feed efficiency has the largest impact on the economics of HT, yielding a NPV after 30 years of $3,511,000 or a 769% ROI. The operating cost of HT is reduced to $0.75/kg live weight due to reduced feed costs (cooling water is assumed to be free and obtained from a subsurface well on-site).

Low-GHG Case (LG). Compared to the BC, the LG operation models several adjustments intended to reduce life cycle GHG emissions of broiler production. LG combines the adjustments from the RE, SC, and HT cases to minimize operational GHG emissions, reduce upstream environmental impacts from corn, and improve feed efficiency. By combining all of these practi- ces, the operating cost of LG is $0.83/kg live weight, which yields a 30-year NPV of $1,927,000, or a 377% ROI. Similar to RE and SC, there are environmental benefits (discussed below). Though the LG scenario does not net the same profit as the BC scenario, increased sales price could be achieved by marketing "sustainable chicken" with reduced GHG emissions and selling the product at a premium sales price, similar to other certi- fied food products (Where Food Comes From Inc, 2021). Alternatively, revenue from GHG reduction credits could incentivize producers to implement the low-GHG practices.
5: <header>Techno-Economic Analysis Results for the Combined Control Volume </header>
Table 4 lists the TEA results and Figure 3 presents a contribution breakdown for operational expenses in all 7 cases relating to the Combined control volume.

Baseline Case (BC). The NPV of the system after 30 years of operation was determined to be $3.06M. This profit represents a 657% ROI (on the down payment, Ceq). During the first 10 years of operation, when loan payments and depreciation are applied, the cumulative discounted cash flow increases from -$404,200 (equity share of capital cost in year 0) to $1,005,100 (year 10). From year 11 to 30, annual gross revenue ($1.70M) exceeds annual operating costs ($1.33M) by $366,700,

resulting in $71,500 of annual tax payments and a steady annual cash flow of $268,800. The largest contrib- utors to the capital costs ($1,347,000) include broiler houses ($1,250,000), land ($26,000), and equipment ($60,000). When summing other costs over the entire 30-year facility lifetime, the largest operating costs include: soybean meal ($18.2M), corn ($11.4M), other feed ingredients ($5.5M), chicks ($0.8M), taxes ($1.8M), heat ($3.2M), electricity ($619,000), loan interest ($278,000), insurance ($396,000), and maintenance ($396,000), bedding ($251,000), and diesel ($45,000) for a total of $42.9M (Figure 2). The annual operating cost ($1.36M) corresponds to $0.77/kg ($0.35/lb) live weight. Revenues from broilers produced and litter sold over the 30-year facility life are $50.2M ($0.97/kg or

$0.45/lb live weight) and $826,000, respectively. The NPV could be increased by obtaining low-cost feed, reducing taxes and labor costs, reducing capital costs for constructing the houses, and/or implementing more effi- cient heating and cooling systems.

Large Farm Case (LF). Compared to the BC, the LF operation is 2 and a half times larger and intended to represent a larger contract operation that can generate better financial returns by enabling economies of scale (MacDonald, 2014). However, all model equations scale linearly with the number of broiler houses as compared to BC (2.5:1). The additional revenue obtained through

the production of more broilers provides a greater NPV ($7.65M) over 30 years. However, because capital invest- ment also increases by a factor of 2.5, the scenario attains the same ROI (657%) over the facility lifespan as the BC.

Large Bird Case (LB). Compared to the BC, the LB operation produces broilers that are approximately one and a half times larger. The initial investment remains the same for LB, but several input factors vary in order to produce larger birds. The LB case has fewer cycles per year (4.7), fewer birds per house (17,800), a higher mortality rate (3.8%), and a higher FCR (2.1 kg

Figure 3. Annual operating expenses and contribution breakdown for each case. Abbreviations: BC, Baseline Case; HT, High Temperature case; LB, Large Bird scenario; LF, Large Farm scenario; LG, Low GHG case; RWN EN, Renewable Energy case; SC, Sustainable Corn scenario.

feed/kg gain). These input factors increase the operating costs to $0.86/kg live weight and reduce the annual yield of broiler live weight by 5.5% (1,626,000 kg/yr). How- ever, the large birds receive a price that is 9% higher than in the BC ($1.06/kg or $0.48/lb), which nearly off- sets the increased production cost. The LB case yields a 30-year NPV of $2,940,000, which is 4% less than the BC and yields a 627% return on investment.

Renewable Energy Case (RWN EN). The RWN EN operation models all on-farm energy inputs from renewable sources, whereby solar power is used for elec- trical and heat requirements and biodiesel replaces con- ventional diesel. The cost of solar power ($0.021/MJ) is based on community scale PV power (Lazard, 2021) and it is assumed that the same amount of heat is required in RWN EN as BC (4,400 GJ/yr). The initial investment is assumed to be the same for the RWN EN case as compared to the BC because the renewable energy is purchased from off-site. Grower operational costs (which include Electricity, Heat, Diesel, and Bedding) total $136,000/yr for BC and $132,000/yr for RWN EN. Though small, this difference in annual energy cost requirements reduces the operating cost for the RWN EN scenario to $0.771/kg live weight (rather than $0.773/kg live weight, as in BC). Because both BC and RWN EN produce the same amount (1,726,000 kg) of live weight per year, RWN EN has a favorable economic outcome when compared to BC. The NPV after 30 years to $3,114,000, or a 670% ROI. The seemingly modest decrease in production cost ($0.002/kg live weight) creates a more profitable scenario over time and reduces environmental impacts (discussed below). This scenario has high sensitivity to the assumed cost of conventional pro- pane and solar-based heat: a 10% change in the cost of conventional propane or solar energy can reverse the economic comparison between the 2 scenarios.

Sustainable Corn Case (SC). The initial invest- ment, broiler performance, and revenue remain the same for SC as for BC, but the increased operating cost for purchasing sustainable corn ($0.29/kg VS. $0.21/kg) reduces the 30-year NPV to $1,356,000, or only a 236% ROI. Due to the increased cost of corn, the cost of pro- duction is increased to $0.85/kg live weight.

High Temperature Sprinkler Cooling Case (HT). Compared to the BC, the HT operation models broiler houses with large-drop sprinkler cooling systems to regulate bird temperature. All of the model variables are the same as BC except for cooling water consump- tion which is reduced by 65% as compared to the cool- cell (0.51 kg H2O/kg live weight), and feed conversion ratio, which is improved by 3.2% (1.80 kg feed/kg live weight). The difference in capital cost for the sprinklers as compared to the cool-cell system is neglected for this study. The improved feed efficiency has the largest impact on the economics of HT, yielding a NPV after 30 years of $3,511,000 or a 769% ROI. The operating cost of HT is reduced to $0.75/kg live weight due to reduced feed costs (cooling water is assumed to be free and obtained from a subsurface well on-site).

Low-GHG Case (LG). Compared to the BC, the LG operation models several adjustments intended to reduce life cycle GHG emissions of broiler production. LG combines the adjustments from the RE, SC, and HT cases to minimize operational GHG emissions, reduce upstream environmental impacts from corn, and improve feed efficiency. By combining all of these practi- ces, the operating cost of LG is $0.83/kg live weight, which yields a 30-year NPV of $1,927,000, or a 377% ROI. Similar to RE and SC, there are environmental benefits (discussed below). Though the LG scenario does not net the same profit as the BC scenario, increased sales price could be achieved by marketing "sustainable chicken" with reduced GHG emissions and selling the product at a premium sales price, similar to other certi- fied food products (Where Food Comes From Inc, 2021). Alternatively, revenue from GHG reduction credits could incentivize producers to implement the low-GHG practices.
7: <header>Techno-Economic Analysis Results for the Combined Control Volume </header>
Table 4 lists the TEA results and Figure 3 presents a contribution breakdown for operational expenses in all 7 cases relating to the Combined control volume.

Baseline Case (BC). The NPV of the system after 30 years of operation was determined to be $3.06M. This profit represents a 657% ROI (on the down payment, Ceq). During the first 10 years of operation, when loan payments and depreciation are applied, the cumulative discounted cash flow increases from -$404,200 (equity share of capital cost in year 0) to $1,005,100 (year 10). From year 11 to 30, annual gross revenue ($1.70M) exceeds annual operating costs ($1.33M) by $366,700,

resulting in $71,500 of annual tax payments and a steady annual cash flow of $268,800. The largest contrib- utors to the capital costs ($1,347,000) include broiler houses ($1,250,000), land ($26,000), and equipment ($60,000). When summing other costs over the entire 30-year facility lifetime, the largest operating costs include: soybean meal ($18.2M), corn ($11.4M), other feed ingredients ($5.5M), chicks ($0.8M), taxes ($1.8M), heat ($3.2M), electricity ($619,000), loan interest ($278,000), insurance ($396,000), and maintenance ($396,000), bedding ($251,000), and diesel ($45,000) for a total of $42.9M (Figure 2). The annual operating cost ($1.36M) corresponds to $0.77/kg ($0.35/lb) live weight. Revenues from broilers produced and litter sold over the 30-year facility life are $50.2M ($0.97/kg or

$0.45/lb live weight) and $826,000, respectively. The NPV could be increased by obtaining low-cost feed, reducing taxes and labor costs, reducing capital costs for constructing the houses, and/or implementing more effi- cient heating and cooling systems.

Large Farm Case (LF). Compared to the BC, the LF operation is 2 and a half times larger and intended to represent a larger contract operation that can generate better financial returns by enabling economies of scale (MacDonald, 2014). However, all model equations scale linearly with the number of broiler houses as compared to BC (2.5:1). The additional revenue obtained through

the production of more broilers provides a greater NPV ($7.65M) over 30 years. However, because capital invest- ment also increases by a factor of 2.5, the scenario attains the same ROI (657%) over the facility lifespan as the BC.

Large Bird Case (LB). Compared to the BC, the LB operation produces broilers that are approximately one and a half times larger. The initial investment remains the same for LB, but several input factors vary in order to produce larger birds. The LB case has fewer cycles per year (4.7), fewer birds per house (17,800), a higher mortality rate (3.8%), and a higher FCR (2.1 kg

Figure 3. Annual operating expenses and contribution breakdown for each case. Abbreviations: BC, Baseline Case; HT, High Temperature case; LB, Large Bird scenario; LF, Large Farm scenario; LG, Low GHG case; RWN EN, Renewable Energy case; SC, Sustainable Corn scenario.

feed/kg gain). These input factors increase the operating costs to $0.86/kg live weight and reduce the annual yield of broiler live weight by 5.5% (1,626,000 kg/yr). How- ever, the large birds receive a price that is 9% higher than in the BC ($1.06/kg or $0.48/lb), which nearly off- sets the increased production cost. The LB case yields a 30-year NPV of $2,940,000, which is 4% less than the BC and yields a 627% return on investment.

Renewable Energy Case (RWN EN). The RWN EN operation models all on-farm energy inputs from renewable sources, whereby solar power is used for elec- trical and heat requirements and biodiesel replaces con- ventional diesel. The cost of solar power ($0.021/MJ) is based on community scale PV power (Lazard, 2021) and it is assumed that the same amount of heat is required in RWN EN as BC (4,400 GJ/yr). The initial investment is assumed to be the same for the RWN EN case as compared to the BC because the renewable energy is purchased from off-site. Grower operational costs (which include Electricity, Heat, Diesel, and Bedding) total $136,000/yr for BC and $132,000/yr for RWN EN. Though small, this difference in annual energy cost requirements reduces the operating cost for the RWN EN scenario to $0.771/kg live weight (rather than $0.773/kg live weight, as in BC). Because both BC and RWN EN produce the same amount (1,726,000 kg) of live weight per year, RWN EN has a favorable economic outcome when compared to BC. The NPV after 30 years to $3,114,000, or a 670% ROI. The seemingly modest decrease in production cost ($0.002/kg live weight) creates a more profitable scenario over time and reduces environmental impacts (discussed below). This scenario has high sensitivity to the assumed cost of conventional pro- pane and solar-based heat: a 10% change in the cost of conventional propane or solar energy can reverse the economic comparison between the 2 scenarios.

Sustainable Corn Case (SC). The initial invest- ment, broiler performance, and revenue remain the same for SC as for BC, but the increased operating cost for purchasing sustainable corn ($0.29/kg VS. $0.21/kg) reduces the 30-year NPV to $1,356,000, or only a 236% ROI. Due to the increased cost of corn, the cost of pro- duction is increased to $0.85/kg live weight.

High Temperature Sprinkler Cooling Case (HT). Compared to the BC, the HT operation models broiler houses with large-drop sprinkler cooling systems to regulate bird temperature. All of the model variables are the same as BC except for cooling water consump- tion which is reduced by 65% as compared to the cool- cell (0.51 kg H2O/kg live weight), and feed conversion ratio, which is improved by 3.2% (1.80 kg feed/kg live weight). The difference in capital cost for the sprinklers as compared to the cool-cell system is neglected for this study. The improved feed efficiency has the largest impact on the economics of HT, yielding a NPV after 30 years of $3,511,000 or a 769% ROI. The operating cost of HT is reduced to $0.75/kg live weight due to reduced feed costs (cooling water is assumed to be free and obtained from a subsurface well on-site).

Low-GHG Case (LG). Compared to the BC, the LG operation models several adjustments intended to reduce life cycle GHG emissions of broiler production. LG combines the adjustments from the RE, SC, and HT cases to minimize operational GHG emissions, reduce upstream environmental impacts from corn, and improve feed efficiency. By combining all of these practi- ces, the operating cost of LG is $0.83/kg live weight, which yields a 30-year NPV of $1,927,000, or a 377% ROI. Similar to RE and SC, there are environmental benefits (discussed below). Though the LG scenario does not net the same profit as the BC scenario, increased sales price could be achieved by marketing "sustainable chicken" with reduced GHG emissions and selling the product at a premium sales price, similar to other certi- fied food products (Where Food Comes From Inc, 2021). Alternatively, revenue from GHG reduction credits could incentivize producers to implement the low-GHG practices.
9: <header>Techno-Economic Analysis Results for the Combined Control Volume </header>
Table 4 lists the TEA results and Figure 3 presents a contribution breakdown for operational expenses in all 7 cases relating to the Combined control volume.

Baseline Case (BC). The NPV of the system after 30 years of operation was determined to be $3.06M. This profit represents a 657% ROI (on the down payment, Ceq). During the first 10 years of operation, when loan payments and depreciation are applied, the cumulative discounted cash flow increases from -$404,200 (equity share of capital cost in year 0) to $1,005,100 (year 10). From year 11 to 30, annual gross revenue ($1.70M) exceeds annual operating costs ($1.33M) by $366,700,

resulting in $71,500 of annual tax payments and a steady annual cash flow of $268,800. The largest contrib- utors to the capital costs ($1,347,000) include broiler houses ($1,250,000), land ($26,000), and equipment ($60,000). When summing other costs over the entire 30-year facility lifetime, the largest operating costs include: soybean meal ($18.2M), corn ($11.4M), other feed ingredients ($5.5M), chicks ($0.8M), taxes ($1.8M), heat ($3.2M), electricity ($619,000), loan interest ($278,000), insurance ($396,000), and maintenance ($396,000), bedding ($251,000), and diesel ($45,000) for a total of $42.9M (Figure 2). The annual operating cost ($1.36M) corresponds to $0.77/kg ($0.35/lb) live weight. Revenues from broilers produced and litter sold over the 30-year facility life are $50.2M ($0.97/kg or

$0.45/lb live weight) and $826,000, respectively. The NPV could be increased by obtaining low-cost feed, reducing taxes and labor costs, reducing capital costs for constructing the houses, and/or implementing more effi- cient heating and cooling systems.

Large Farm Case (LF). Compared to the BC, the LF operation is 2 and a half times larger and intended to represent a larger contract operation that can generate better financial returns by enabling economies of scale (MacDonald, 2014). However, all model equations scale linearly with the number of broiler houses as compared to BC (2.5:1). The additional revenue obtained through

the production of more broilers provides a greater NPV ($7.65M) over 30 years. However, because capital invest- ment also increases by a factor of 2.5, the scenario attains the same ROI (657%) over the facility lifespan as the BC.

Large Bird Case (LB). Compared to the BC, the LB operation produces broilers that are approximately one and a half times larger. The initial investment remains the same for LB, but several input factors vary in order to produce larger birds. The LB case has fewer cycles per year (4.7), fewer birds per house (17,800), a higher mortality rate (3.8%), and a higher FCR (2.1 kg

Figure 3. Annual operating expenses and contribution breakdown for each case. Abbreviations: BC, Baseline Case; HT, High Temperature case; LB, Large Bird scenario; LF, Large Farm scenario; LG, Low GHG case; RWN EN, Renewable Energy case; SC, Sustainable Corn scenario.

feed/kg gain). These input factors increase the operating costs to $0.86/kg live weight and reduce the annual yield of broiler live weight by 5.5% (1,626,000 kg/yr). How- ever, the large birds receive a price that is 9% higher than in the BC ($1.06/kg or $0.48/lb), which nearly off- sets the increased production cost. The LB case yields a 30-year NPV of $2,940,000, which is 4% less than the BC and yields a 627% return on investment.

Renewable Energy Case (RWN EN). The RWN EN operation models all on-farm energy inputs from renewable sources, whereby solar power is used for elec- trical and heat requirements and biodiesel replaces con- ventional diesel. The cost of solar power ($0.021/MJ) is based on community scale PV power (Lazard, 2021) and it is assumed that the same amount of heat is required in RWN EN as BC (4,400 GJ/yr). The initial investment is assumed to be the same for the RWN EN case as compared to the BC because the renewable energy is purchased from off-site. Grower operational costs (which include Electricity, Heat, Diesel, and Bedding) total $136,000/yr for BC and $132,000/yr for RWN EN. Though small, this difference in annual energy cost requirements reduces the operating cost for the RWN EN scenario to $0.771/kg live weight (rather than $0.773/kg live weight, as in BC). Because both BC and RWN EN produce the same amount (1,726,000 kg) of live weight per year, RWN EN has a favorable economic outcome when compared to BC. The NPV after 30 years to $3,114,000, or a 670% ROI. The seemingly modest decrease in production cost ($0.002/kg live weight) creates a more profitable scenario over time and reduces environmental impacts (discussed below). This scenario has high sensitivity to the assumed cost of conventional pro- pane and solar-based heat: a 10% change in the cost of conventional propane or solar energy can reverse the economic comparison between the 2 scenarios.

Sustainable Corn Case (SC). The initial invest- ment, broiler performance, and revenue remain the same for SC as for BC, but the increased operating cost for purchasing sustainable corn ($0.29/kg VS. $0.21/kg) reduces the 30-year NPV to $1,356,000, or only a 236% ROI. Due to the increased cost of corn, the cost of pro- duction is increased to $0.85/kg live weight.

High Temperature Sprinkler Cooling Case (HT). Compared to the BC, the HT operation models broiler houses with large-drop sprinkler cooling systems to regulate bird temperature. All of the model variables are the same as BC except for cooling water consump- tion which is reduced by 65% as compared to the cool- cell (0.51 kg H2O/kg live weight), and feed conversion ratio, which is improved by 3.2% (1.80 kg feed/kg live weight). The difference in capital cost for the sprinklers as compared to the cool-cell system is neglected for this study. The improved feed efficiency has the largest impact on the economics of HT, yielding a NPV after 30 years of $3,511,000 or a 769% ROI. The operating cost of HT is reduced to $0.75/kg live weight due to reduced feed costs (cooling water is assumed to be free and obtained from a subsurface well on-site).

Low-GHG Case (LG). Compared to the BC, the LG operation models several adjustments intended to reduce life cycle GHG emissions of broiler production. LG combines the adjustments from the RE, SC, and HT cases to minimize operational GHG emissions, reduce upstream environmental impacts from corn, and improve feed efficiency. By combining all of these practi- ces, the operating cost of LG is $0.83/kg live weight, which yields a 30-year NPV of $1,927,000, or a 377% ROI. Similar to RE and SC, there are environmental benefits (discussed below). Though the LG scenario does not net the same profit as the BC scenario, increased sales price could be achieved by marketing "sustainable chicken" with reduced GHG emissions and selling the product at a premium sales price, similar to other certi- fied food products (Where Food Comes From Inc, 2021). Alternatively, revenue from GHG reduction credits could incentivize producers to implement the low-GHG practices.
11: <header>Techno-Economic Analysis Results for the Combined Control Volume </header>
Table 4 lists the TEA results and Figure 3 presents a contribution breakdown for operational expenses in all 7 cases relating to the Combined control volume.

Baseline Case (BC). The NPV of the system after 30 years of operation was determined to be $3.06M. This profit represents a 657% ROI (on the down payment, Ceq). During the first 10 years of operation, when loan payments and depreciation are applied, the cumulative discounted cash flow increases from -$404,200 (equity share of capital cost in year 0) to $1,005,100 (year 10). From year 11 to 30, annual gross revenue ($1.70M) exceeds annual operating costs ($1.33M) by $366,700,

resulting in $71,500 of annual tax payments and a steady annual cash flow of $268,800. The largest contrib- utors to the capital costs ($1,347,000) include broiler houses ($1,250,000), land ($26,000), and equipment ($60,000). When summing other costs over the entire 30-year facility lifetime, the largest operating costs include: soybean meal ($18.2M), corn ($11.4M), other feed ingredients ($5.5M), chicks ($0.8M), taxes ($1.8M), heat ($3.2M), electricity ($619,000), loan interest ($278,000), insurance ($396,000), and maintenance ($396,000), bedding ($251,000), and diesel ($45,000) for a total of $42.9M (Figure 2). The annual operating cost ($1.36M) corresponds to $0.77/kg ($0.35/lb) live weight. Revenues from broilers produced and litter sold over the 30-year facility life are $50.2M ($0.97/kg or

$0.45/lb live weight) and $826,000, respectively. The NPV could be increased by obtaining low-cost feed, reducing taxes and labor costs, reducing capital costs for constructing the houses, and/or implementing more effi- cient heating and cooling systems.

Large Farm Case (LF). Compared to the BC, the LF operation is 2 and a half times larger and intended to represent a larger contract operation that can generate better financial returns by enabling economies of scale (MacDonald, 2014). However, all model equations scale linearly with the number of broiler houses as compared to BC (2.5:1). The additional revenue obtained through

the production of more broilers provides a greater NPV ($7.65M) over 30 years. However, because capital invest- ment also increases by a factor of 2.5, the scenario attains the same ROI (657%) over the facility lifespan as the BC.

Large Bird Case (LB). Compared to the BC, the LB operation produces broilers that are approximately one and a half times larger. The initial investment remains the same for LB, but several input factors vary in order to produce larger birds. The LB case has fewer cycles per year (4.7), fewer birds per house (17,800), a higher mortality rate (3.8%), and a higher FCR (2.1 kg

Figure 3. Annual operating expenses and contribution breakdown for each case. Abbreviations: BC, Baseline Case; HT, High Temperature case; LB, Large Bird scenario; LF, Large Farm scenario; LG, Low GHG case; RWN EN, Renewable Energy case; SC, Sustainable Corn scenario.

feed/kg gain). These input factors increase the operating costs to $0.86/kg live weight and reduce the annual yield of broiler live weight by 5.5% (1,626,000 kg/yr). How- ever, the large birds receive a price that is 9% higher than in the BC ($1.06/kg or $0.48/lb), which nearly off- sets the increased production cost. The LB case yields a 30-year NPV of $2,940,000, which is 4% less than the BC and yields a 627% return on investment.

Renewable Energy Case (RWN EN). The RWN EN operation models all on-farm energy inputs from renewable sources, whereby solar power is used for elec- trical and heat requirements and biodiesel replaces con- ventional diesel. The cost of solar power ($0.021/MJ) is based on community scale PV power (Lazard, 2021) and it is assumed that the same amount of heat is required in RWN EN as BC (4,400 GJ/yr). The initial investment is assumed to be the same for the RWN EN case as compared to the BC because the renewable energy is purchased from off-site. Grower operational costs (which include Electricity, Heat, Diesel, and Bedding) total $136,000/yr for BC and $132,000/yr for RWN EN. Though small, this difference in annual energy cost requirements reduces the operating cost for the RWN EN scenario to $0.771/kg live weight (rather than $0.773/kg live weight, as in BC). Because both BC and RWN EN produce the same amount (1,726,000 kg) of live weight per year, RWN EN has a favorable economic outcome when compared to BC. The NPV after 30 years to $3,114,000, or a 670% ROI. The seemingly modest decrease in production cost ($0.002/kg live weight) creates a more profitable scenario over time and reduces environmental impacts (discussed below). This scenario has high sensitivity to the assumed cost of conventional pro- pane and solar-based heat: a 10% change in the cost of conventional propane or solar energy can reverse the economic comparison between the 2 scenarios.

Sustainable Corn Case (SC). The initial invest- ment, broiler performance, and revenue remain the same for SC as for BC, but the increased operating cost for purchasing sustainable corn ($0.29/kg VS. $0.21/kg) reduces the 30-year NPV to $1,356,000, or only a 236% ROI. Due to the increased cost of corn, the cost of pro- duction is increased to $0.85/kg live weight.

High Temperature Sprinkler Cooling Case (HT). Compared to the BC, the HT operation models broiler houses with large-drop sprinkler cooling systems to regulate bird temperature. All of the model variables are the same as BC except for cooling water consump- tion which is reduced by 65% as compared to the cool- cell (0.51 kg H2O/kg live weight), and feed conversion ratio, which is improved by 3.2% (1.80 kg feed/kg live weight). The difference in capital cost for the sprinklers as compared to the cool-cell system is neglected for this study. The improved feed efficiency has the largest impact on the economics of HT, yielding a NPV after 30 years of $3,511,000 or a 769% ROI. The operating cost of HT is reduced to $0.75/kg live weight due to reduced feed costs (cooling water is assumed to be free and obtained from a subsurface well on-site).

Low-GHG Case (LG). Compared to the BC, the LG operation models several adjustments intended to reduce life cycle GHG emissions of broiler production. LG combines the adjustments from the RE, SC, and HT cases to minimize operational GHG emissions, reduce upstream environmental impacts from corn, and improve feed efficiency. By combining all of these practi- ces, the operating cost of LG is $0.83/kg live weight, which yields a 30-year NPV of $1,927,000, or a 377% ROI. Similar to RE and SC, there are environmental benefits (discussed below). Though the LG scenario does not net the same profit as the BC scenario, increased sales price could be achieved by marketing "sustainable chicken" with reduced GHG emissions and selling the product at a premium sales price, similar to other certi- fied food products (Where Food Comes From Inc, 2021). Alternatively, revenue from GHG reduction credits could incentivize producers to implement the low-GHG practices.

Table 4 lists the TEA results and Figure 3 presents a contribution breakdown for operational expenses in all 7 cases relating to the Combined control volume.

Baseline Case (BC). The NPV of the system after 30 years of operation was determined to be $3.06M. This profit represents a 657% ROI (on the down payment, Ceq). During the first 10 years of operation, when loan payments and depreciation are applied, the cumulative discounted cash flow increases from -$404,200 (equity share of capital cost in year 0) to $1,005,100 (year 10). From year 11 to 30, annual gross revenue ($1.70M) exceeds annual operating costs ($1.33M) by $366,700,

resulting in $71,500 of annual tax payments and a steady annual cash flow of $268,800. The largest contrib- utors to the capital costs ($1,347,000) include broiler houses ($1,250,000), land ($26,000), and equipment ($60,000). When summing other costs over the entire 30-year facility lifetime, the largest operating costs include: soybean meal ($18.2M), corn ($11.4M), other feed ingredients ($5.5M), chicks ($0.8M), taxes ($1.8M), heat ($3.2M), electricity ($619,000), loan interest ($278,000), insurance ($396,000), and maintenance ($396,000), bedding ($251,000), and diesel ($45,000) for a total of $42.9M (Figure 2). The annual operating cost ($1.36M) corresponds to $0.77/kg ($0.35/lb) live weight. Revenues from broilers produced and litter sold over the 30-year facility life are $50.2M ($0.97/kg or

$0.45/lb live weight) and $826,000, respectively. The NPV could be increased by obtaining low-cost feed, reducing taxes and labor costs, reducing capital costs for constructing the houses, and/or implementing more effi- cient heating and cooling systems.

Large Farm Case (LF). Compared to the BC, the LF operation is 2 and a half times larger and intended to represent a larger contract operation that can generate better financial returns by enabling economies of scale (MacDonald, 2014). However, all model equations scale linearly with the number of broiler houses as compared to BC (2.5:1). The additional revenue obtained through

the production of more broilers provides a greater NPV ($7.65M) over 30 years. However, because capital invest- ment also increases by a factor of 2.5, the scenario attains the same ROI (657%) over the facility lifespan as the BC.

Large Bird Case (LB). Compared to the BC, the LB operation produces broilers that are approximately one and a half times larger. The initial investment remains the same for LB, but several input factors vary in order to produce larger birds. The LB case has fewer cycles per year (4.7), fewer birds per house (17,800), a higher mortality rate (3.8%), and a higher FCR (2.1 kg

Figure 3. Annual operating expenses and contribution breakdown for each case. Abbreviations: BC, Baseline Case; HT, High Temperature case; LB, Large Bird scenario; LF, Large Farm scenario; LG, Low GHG case; RWN EN, Renewable Energy case; SC, Sustainable Corn scenario.

feed/kg gain). These input factors increase the operating costs to $0.86/kg live weight and reduce the annual yield of broiler live weight by 5.5% (1,626,000 kg/yr). How- ever, the large birds receive a price that is 9% higher than in the BC ($1.06/kg or $0.48/lb), which nearly off- sets the increased production cost. The LB case yields a 30-year NPV of $2,940,000, which is 4% less than the BC and yields a 627% return on investment.

Renewable Energy Case (RWN EN). The RWN EN operation models all on-farm energy inputs from renewable sources, whereby solar power is used for elec- trical and heat requirements and biodiesel replaces con- ventional diesel. The cost of solar power ($0.021/MJ) is based on community scale PV power (Lazard, 2021) and it is assumed that the same amount of heat is required in RWN EN as BC (4,400 GJ/yr). The initial investment is assumed to be the same for the RWN EN case as compared to the BC because the renewable energy is purchased from off-site. Grower operational costs (which include Electricity, Heat, Diesel, and Bedding) total $136,000/yr for BC and $132,000/yr for RWN EN. Though small, this difference in annual energy cost requirements reduces the operating cost for the RWN EN scenario to $0.771/kg live weight (rather than $0.773/kg live weight, as in BC). Because both BC and RWN EN produce the same amount (1,726,000 kg) of live weight per year, RWN EN has a favorable economic outcome when compared to BC. The NPV after 30 years to $3,114,000, or a 670% ROI. The seemingly modest decrease in production cost ($0.002/kg live weight) creates a more profitable scenario over time and reduces environmental impacts (discussed below). This scenario has high sensitivity to the assumed cost of conventional pro- pane and solar-based heat: a 10% change in the cost of conventional propane or solar energy can reverse the economic comparison between the 2 scenarios.

Sustainable Corn Case (SC). The initial invest- ment, broiler performance, and revenue remain the same for SC as for BC, but the increased operating cost for purchasing sustainable corn ($0.29/kg VS. $0.21/kg) reduces the 30-year NPV to $1,356,000, or only a 236% ROI. Due to the increased cost of corn, the cost of pro- duction is increased to $0.85/kg live weight.

High Temperature Sprinkler Cooling Case (HT). Compared to the BC, the HT operation models broiler houses with large-drop sprinkler cooling systems to regulate bird temperature. All of the model variables are the same as BC except for cooling water consump- tion which is reduced by 65% as compared to the cool- cell (0.51 kg H2O/kg live weight), and feed conversion ratio, which is improved by 3.2% (1.80 kg feed/kg live weight). The difference in capital cost for the sprinklers as compared to the cool-cell system is neglected for this study. The improved feed efficiency has the largest impact on the economics of HT, yielding a NPV after 30 years of $3,511,000 or a 769% ROI. The operating cost of HT is reduced to $0.75/kg live weight due to reduced feed costs (cooling water is assumed to be free and obtained from a subsurface well on-site).

Low-GHG Case (LG). Compared to the BC, the LG operation models several adjustments intended to reduce life cycle GHG emissions of broiler production. LG combines the adjustments from the RE, SC, and HT cases to minimize operational GHG emissions, reduce upstream environmental impacts from corn, and improve feed efficiency. By combining all of these practi- ces, the operating cost of LG is $0.83/kg live weight, which yields a 30-year NPV of $1,927,000, or a 377% ROI. Similar to RE and SC, there are environmental benefits (discussed below). Though the LG scenario does not net the same profit as the BC scenario, increased sales price could be achieved by marketing "sustainable chicken" with reduced GHG emissions and selling the product at a premium sales price, similar to other certi- fied food products (Where Food Comes From Inc, 2021). Alternatively, revenue from GHG reduction credits could incentivize producers to implement the low-GHG practices.

From year 11 to 30, annual gross revenue ($1.70M) exceeds annual operating costs ($1.33M) by $366,700,

resulting in $71,500 of annual tax payments and a steady annual cash flow of $268,800.
