Table 4 details the input and output flows associated with the baseline case, Table 5 summarizes the LCA results, and Table 6 presents a summary of direct emis- sions. Figure 4 displays a contribution breakdown of the GHG contributors to the baseline case. Finally, Figures 5 and 6 illustrate a contribution breakdown for each LCA indicator for all 7 cases relating to the Combined control volume.

Baseline Case (BC). The primary contributions to GHG emissions, fossil CED, and RE (Figures 5A and 5B and 6B) are corn, soybean meal, heat, chick production, electricity, emissions from manure (which are detailed in Tables 4 and 6), and dicalcium phosphate. WDP, ALOP, and EUT (Figures 5C and 5D and 6A) are domi- nated by upstream impacts from corn and soy produc- tion, followed by chicks and corn oil production.

Large Farm Case (LF). The LCA impacts of LF are identical to BC because all LCA impact categories in this model ultimately scale proportionally to the number of birds/houses on the farm; because there are 2.5x more houses, there are 2.5x more impacts (numerator), but also 2.5x more live weight produced (denominator).

Large Bird Case (LB). All LCA impacts are higher for LB than BC by 8% to 13%, primarily due to a lower total annual live weight yield (reduces the denomina- tor). The reduced live weight is due to fewer birds per house and a slightly higher mortality rate than BC. Additionally, the larger birds have a marginally higher FCR than in the BC scenario. These factors increase the impacts associated with feed and manure per kg of gain (increases numerator).

Renewable Energy Case (RWN EN). The GHG, CED, and RWN EN impacts for the RWN EN scenario are 14% to 27% lower than those for BC as a result of reducing the negative impacts of fossil fuels, while impacts from feed production and manure are
Table 5. TEA/LCA summary results for all 7 production systems.
Net Present Value. Return on Investment. Greenhouse Gas impact. Cumulative Energy Demand. Water Depletion Potential. 6 Agricultural Land Occupation. Eutrophication. Respiratory Effects.
unchanged. WDP, ALOP, and EUT outcomes for the RWN EN case are very similar to those for BC. The net GHG emissions for the RWN EN case are 0.23 kg COe/ kg live weight lower than BC.

Sustainable Corn Case (SC). The environmental impacts of corn production are the largest or second- largest contribution to all LCA indicators in BC. By replacing conventional corn with "sustainable corn" in this model, 5-out-of-6 LCA indicators are reduced by 10% to 77%. Reductions for WDP (82%) and EUT (37%) are most notable in SC, as these environmental impacts of broiler production are dominated by the irri- gation water demand and nitrogen fertilizer associated with conventional corn production, respectively. GHG, CED, and RE are also reduced by 12% to 19% as a result

of sustainable corn being produced using biodiesel fuel and reduced nitrogen fertilizer. The ALOP increased for SC by 8% as compared to BC due to the reduced corn yield per acre assumed for the SC scenario.

High Temperature Sprinkler Cooling System Case (HT). The HT scenario reduced all LCA impacts by 2% to 3% compared to BC, which is almost entirely due to the increased feed efficiency assumed for this case in the model. Although the amount of cooling water is reduced by 65% in HT as compared to BC, the direct water use for cool- ing on a broiler farm is a negligible contribution to the over- all WDP, as compared to the WDP resulting from irrigated corn production (more than 480x larger).
Low-GHG Case (LG). The LG case combines sus- tainability benefits from RE, SC, and HT to generate

Figure 4. Breakdown of GHG emission contributions for the Baseline Case in t CO2e over 30 years. Combined GHG emissions in the Baseline Case are 1.31 kg COe/kg live weight, which is similar to that found in prior assessments (Pelletier, 2008; FAO, 2013; Wernet et al., 2016; Putman et al., 2017; Thoma, 2021).

Large Farm Case (LF). The LCA impacts of LF are identical to BC because all LCA impact categories in this model ultimately scale proportionally to the number of birds/houses on the farm; because there are 2.5x more houses, there are 2.5x more impacts (numerator), but also 2.5x more live weight produced (denominator).

Large Bird Case (LB). All LCA impacts are higher for LB than BC by 8% to 13%, primarily due to a lower total annual live weight yield (reduces the denomina- tor). 2: <header>TEAILCA Results Comparison for Combined, Integrator, and Grower Control Volumes </header>
The Combined control volume includes contributions from the Integrator (see Appendix A) and Grower (see Appendix B) that are not shared equally. Modeled

Grower revenues (~$0.18/kg live weight, from contractor payments and litter sales) and production costs (~$0.08/ kg live weight, for on-site energy) are sufficient to repay the capital investment when considering all economic parameters (interest payments, discount rate accounting for the time-value-of-money, etc.) over a 30-year facility life and net a positive ROI (greater than 60% in all cases). Growers tend to break-even on investment between 20 and 22 years: the median NPVs for Growers and Integrators after 30 years are $689,000 and $2,300,000, respectively. The Integrator makes no capital investment in this model and is able to generate substan- tial profit from selling broilers (~$0.97/kg live weight) after all production costs are accounted for (~$0.86/kg live weight). However, the Integrator also assumes feed and product risks, among others, on behalf of the Grower in Production Contract arrangements (Knoeber, 1995).
Table 6. Methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20) emissions from broiler manure in the Baseline Case in units of kg CO2e/kg live weight.

|Value Manure CH4 emissions, poultry house|0.021 Direct nitrous oxide emissions, poultry house|0.020 Indirect nitrous oxide emissions, volatilization, poultry house|0.041 Indirect nitrous oxide emissions, leaching, poultry house|0.000 Total manure greenhouse gas emissions|0.081
Litter spreading is not included in the control volume and therefore does not impact the broiler production impacts.

For instance, if the modeled unit costs of both corn and soybean meal are increased by 20%, the Integrator fails to return a profit in 6-of-7 scenarios and the Grower remains unaffected. While the Grower typically achieves a lower profit than the Integrator, they are insulated from feed price volatility and thus may incur lower eco- nomic risk (MacDonald, 2014).

For the LCA impacts, correlations were found between the GHG, CED, and RE indicators and the WDP, ALOP, and EUT indicators. Generally speaking, the GHG, CED, and RE indicators have roughly 65% to 80% of their contributions from upstream feed production (which include upstream fuels used to grow corn, soy, etc., manufacturing of nitrogen fertilizer, etc.) and 35% from on-site fuels. As a result, the Integrator is responsi- ble for a larger share (~65%-80%) of these impacts than the Grower (~20%-35%). On the other hand, WDP, ALOP, and EUT are almost entirely driven by upstream feed production, and therefore the Integrator is responsi- ble for nearly all of these environmental burdens.

Grower revenues (~$0.18/kg live weight, from contractor payments and litter sales) and production costs (~$0.08/ kg live weight, for on-site energy) are sufficient to repay the capital investment when considering all economic parameters (interest payments, discount rate accounting for the time-value-of-money, etc.) over a 30-year facility life and net a positive ROI (greater than 60% in all cases). Growers tend to break-even on investment between 20 and 22 years: the median NPVs for Growers and Integrators after 30 years are $689,000 and $2,300,000, respectively. The Integrator makes no capital investment in this model and is able to generate substan- tial profit from selling broilers (~$0.97/kg live weight) after all production costs are accounted for (~$0.86/kg live weight). However, the Integrator also assumes feed and product risks, among others, on behalf of the Grower in Production Contract arrangements (Knoeber, 1995).

Litter spreading is not included in the control volume and therefore does not impact the broiler production impacts.

For instance, if the modeled unit costs of both corn and soybean meal are increased by 20%, the Integrator fails to return a profit in 6-of-7 scenarios and the Grower remains unaffected. While the Grower typically achieves a lower profit than the Integrator, they are insulated from feed price volatility and thus may incur lower eco- nomic risk (MacDonald, 2014).

For the LCA impacts, correlations were found between the GHG, CED, and RE indicators and the WDP, ALOP, and EUT indicators. Generally speaking, the GHG, CED, and RE indicators have roughly 65% to 80% of their contributions from upstream feed production (which include upstream fuels used to grow corn, soy, etc., manufacturing of nitrogen fertilizer, etc.) and 35% from on-site fuels. As a result, the Integrator is responsi- ble for a larger share (~65%-80%) of these impacts than the Grower (~20%-35%). On the other hand, WDP, ALOP, and EUT are almost entirely driven by upstream feed production, and therefore the Integrator is responsi- ble for nearly all of these environmental burdens.

6: Sensitivity Analysis results are presented in Figure 7 and A.1 (Appendix). Several model performance param- eters were tested and include Mortality Rate, FCR, Corn Upstream Impact, Soybean Meal Upstream Impacts, Electricity Upstream Impacts, and Propane Upstream Impacts. Additionally, 5 economic-only parameters were tested and include Capital Expendi- ture, Discount/Tax/Interest Rate, House Size, Bird Density, and Live Broiler Sale Price. Finally, 2 parame- ters are tested as scenarios (rather than with a range of inputs) and include a 20-year Life-Span case and a Corn Ration case. These scenarios (i.e., the 20-year and corn- as-feed cases) are discussed briefly. Appendix C of the SI contains a more detailed discussion of the remaining Sensitivity results.

Twenty-Year Life Span Scenario: Environmental results of the 20-year Life Span case are the same as the BC as the physical and energy flows do not change from year-to-year. Economically, the NPV ($2.3M) and ROI (464%) are still favorable but reduced when compared to the 30-year term. In the 20-year Life Span case, the Grower's final NPV is $318,000.

Corn Ration Scenario: The economic results for the Corn Ration scenario are overwhelmingly favorable, as the price of corn ($0.21/kg) is less than the price of soy- bean ($0.29/kg). The NPV after 30 years is $3.7M and corresponds to an ROI of 827%. Environmental results of the scenario are poor when compared to the BC; 6-of- 7 categories have a higher impact. Only ALOP is improved in the Corn Ration scenario, due to the rela- tive land intensities between corn and soy.

1: This study demonstrates several important character- istics of the U.S. broiler industry. In general, broiler pro- duction in the United States has been largely optimized for economic interests, yielding a tremendous amount of chicken meat with reasonable margins. These margins are highly dependent on feed costs and broiler sale pri- ces; a difference of just a few cents can translate into mil- lions of dollars of profit/loss. The economic opportunities currently appear stronger for Integrators than contract Growers, though Integrators assume more risk to market fluctuations (MacDonald, 2022).

The overall environmental burden of broiler produc- tion is shared by Integrators and Growers, primarily driven by upstream feed production (Integrators' responsibility) and on-farm energy consumption (Growers' responsibility). We found that the Large Farm, Large Bird, and High Temperature scenarios had small or no LCA improvements over the Baseline Case, while the Renewable Energy, Sustainable Corn, and Low-GHG cases demonstrated significant sustainabil- ity benefits-albeit with increased production costs. For instance, the LG case reduced GHG emissions by 38% as compared to BC, which corresponds to a CO2 avoidance cost of $23/t CO2e, which is cheaper than many pro- posed negative emissions technologies (Sanz-Pérez et al., 2016; Smith et al., 2016). Perhaps more importantly, the LG case achieve reductions of 33% to 83% over the BC for 5-out-of-6 LCA indicators, and the SC case generated large reductions in WDP (82%) and EUT (37%).

Different regions of the United States-and in fact dif- ferent farms within the same region-will have different energy and cost requirements, climate conditions, and management practices, which influence the TEA/LCA results. The sensitivity analysis illustrates the potential variability of the sustainability impacts evaluated in this study for several critical parameters related to man- agement practices and performance. In future work, we intend to include a geospatial component to the model that will enable comparisons of broiler farm performance depending on regional differences in feed costs, energy demand, energy costs, capital costs, and upstream envi- ronmental performance. This future work can build upon this study and the county-by-county assessment of corn and soy production presented by Smith et al. (2023). In particular, it will be valuable to compare TEA/LCA results for the leading poultry-producing regions, such as Georgia, Alabama, and Arkansas (US Department of Agriculture, N. A. S. S., 2023).

Based on these results, we conclude that although the US broiler industry has largely optimized production economics, there are significant opportunities to improve environmental sustainability. On a national scale (27 billion kg of broiler live weight per year), implementing the LG scenario would avoid: 13.5 million tonnes of CO2e (~0.03% of US GHG emissions), 123 GJ of fossil energy demand, 6.6 billion m² 3 of water depletion, 13.1 billion kg N-eq eutrophication, and 12.7 billion kg PM 2.5-eq, with an increase in agricultural land use of 3.4

billion m². Broiler producers (i.e., the ~20 integrators that are responsible for over 95% of the US broiler meat production) have several options to monetize environ- mental improvements, including premiums for chicken products that appeal to environmentally conscious con- sumers and the recently launched USDA Climate Smart programs (USDA, 2022).
